advantages: Principled objective: pick θ that makes observed data most probable under an explicit model—unifies many ML loss functions.
Log-likelihood converts products to sums, enabling stable computation and per-example gradients for large datasets.
Often yields closed-form estimators on simple models (sample mean, sample proportion) with clear intuition.
Asymptotically efficient under standard conditions—MLE uses information in the data effectively at large n.
Direct bridge from probabilistic modeling to optimization: −ℓ(θ) plugs into gradient descent and autograd frameworks.
disadvantages: Can overfit small samples or hit parameter boundaries (e.g. p̂ = 0 or 1) without regularization or priors.
Assumes the parametric model is correctly specified—wrong likelihood family yields biased or misleading θ̂.
No built-in uncertainty quantification on finite data; point estimate only unless supplemented (Fisher information, bootstrap, Bayes).
Closed-form MLE may be biased on finite samples (Gaussian σ² with divisor n) or unavailable for complex models.
Sensitive to outliers under Gaussian assumptions; heavy-tailed or robust likelihoods may be preferable.
